Rafael, the proposal is to open a second office in the south. What is your point of view?
In my opinion, this is the right moment. I think the new clients there need a local office.
I see your point, but I have a concern. The cost is very high this year.
That is a reasonable concern. Actually, we can start with a small office and grow later.
That is a good compromise. I agree with that plan. Shall we vote?
Yes, let us vote. If most of us approve, we open the small office in the south.

Good morning, everyone, and thank you for coming to the board meeting. We have one main decision today. The proposal is to open a second office in the south of the country. In my opinion, this is the right moment, because revenue has grown and we have new clients there. However, I understand the concern about the cost. So here is the plan. First, each director will share a point of view. Then we will discuss the budget. Finally, we will vote. If most of us approve, we move forward. If not, we will look for a reasonable compromise. Let us keep the discussion open and respectful. Every opinion matters at this table.

When I started my career, I was afraid to disagree in meetings. I thought a good director always says yes. But over the years, I have learned something important. A board does not need people who agree with everything. It needs honest points of view. When I believe a proposal is wrong, I say it with respect. I say, I see your point, but I think there is a risk here. I never attack the person. I question the idea. And when a colleague gives a strong reason, I change my mind. That is not weakness. That is how a good decision is born. The best boards disagree well and then decide together.

First, the chair opens the meeting and presents the proposal. Then, each director shares a point of view about the idea. Next, the board discusses the main concern, which is the cost. After that, the directors look for a reasonable compromise. Finally, the board votes, and the majority approves the new plan.
